title: Service Outcomes, Boundaries, and Server Lifecycle · Concurrent Code Workshop
summary: A server pairing session predicts request, handler, dependency, response, and cleanup transitions before building the first service outcome, boundary, and lifecycle map for service outcomes, boundaries, and server lifecycle. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of service outcome, boundary, and lifecycle map while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Define a server outcome with user, client, operator, security, and accessibility evidence instead of treating endpoint count as success.
Trace user agent, client, proxy, gateway, origin handler, dependency, and response roles without assigning one component evidence it cannot observe.
Distinguish process, listener, connection, stream, request, handler, goroutine, dependency, and durable-state lifetimes.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Service Outcome Contract
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop team handles case 9360 in a civic incident API behind one trusted regional proxy. Build deterministic Go evidence to separate listener liveness, dependency readiness, and draining state; then cancel during the dependency phase and preserve cleanup plus response ownership. Browser code models decisions only. Listener, TLS, database, proxy, race, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Service Outcome Contract.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for define a server outcome with user, client, operator, security, and accessibility evidence instead of treating endpoint count as success. For Go server Commit to the mission model · Workshop Service Outcome Contract, keep Service Outcome Contract connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

steps.why: Retrieving Lifecycle Evidence Ownership through the concrete service outcome, boundary, and lifecycle map case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Lifecycle Evidence Ownership, keep Lifecycle Evidence Ownership connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Assign startup, request, dependency, shutdown, rollback, alert, and data-recovery evidence to named owners and done conditions.

hints: For Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Lifecycle Evidence Ownership, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Lifecycle Evidence Ownership precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Lifecycle Evidence Ownership, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: A workshop team handles case 9369 in a recovery rehearsal with an ambiguous database commit. Build deterministic Go evidence to separate listener liveness, dependency readiness, and draining state; then saturate one admission budget while another tenant remains serviceable. Browser code models decisions only. Listener, TLS, database, proxy, race, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Assign startup, request, dependency, shutdown, rollback, alert, and data-recovery evidence to named owners and done conditions.

For Go server Defend the transferred model · Workshop Lifecycle Evidence Ownership, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: A 200 health response is a complete operational ownership and recovery plan.
